ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef yakiniku bowl; closest parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 term exists for yakiniku bowl as a composite dish; 'Beef, cooked' (A0CKP) used as closest parent anchor for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for beef yakiniku bowl as a composite prepared dish.

main_ingredients: yakiniku sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, sesame)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g of grilled beef with yakiniku sauce and 250 g of cooked Japanese short-grain white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Yakiniku Bowl — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, grilled (NDB 23620) and Cooked white rice, short-grain (NDB 20444), with yakiniku sauce macros estimated from USDA Branded Food sauce reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for beef yakiniku bowl as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled lean beef and cooked short-grain white rice, combined in a ratio of approximately 37.5% beef and 62.5% rice by weight (150 g beef : 250 g rice per 400 g serving), with yakiniku sauce contribution to sugar and sodium incorporated from USDA Branded Food sauce reference data. Per-100 g values are weighted composites of these components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, short-grain, cooked (GI ~72); composite bowl GI dominated by white rice component
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef yakiniku bowl as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source — Japanese short-grain white rice — which has a published GI of approximately 72 (glucose reference) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The beef and yakiniku sauce components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 98 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 70.6.

hypertension.guidance: At approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1240 mg per standard 400 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals and should be consumed infrequently or with a reduced-sauce modification.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and large carbohydrate load from white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high GI of 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 70.6 per standard serving make this dish a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion reduction, substitution of brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is recommended to moderate glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from beef and yakiniku sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 1.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from the yakiniku sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yakiniku Bowl (牛焼肉丼, Gyū Yakiniku Don) is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of thinly sliced beef that has been marinated and grilled in a sweet-savory yakiniku sauce, served over a bed of warm Japanese short-grain white rice. Yakiniku sauce is typically made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, and sesame, imparting a glossy, umami-rich coating to the beef. The dish is a staple of Japanese gyūdon-style fast-casual dining and is widely served at chains such as Sukiya across Japan and Singapore. The texture contrasts tender, slightly caramelised grilled beef with soft, sticky Japanese rice. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in protein (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g) and relatively high in carbohydrate (approximately 24.5 g per 100 g) due to the white rice base, with a high glycemic index of approximately 72 driven by the short-grain rice. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to the soy-based sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for individuals with soy or wheat allergies due to the sauce composition. It is dairy-free and suitable for non-vegetarian diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef yakiniku bowl?
What is yakiniku sauce made of?
Is beef yakiniku bowl high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat beef yakiniku bowl?
What is the GI of beef yakiniku bowl?
Is beef yakiniku bowl gluten free?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for beef yakiniku bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled lean beef and cooked short-grain white rice combined in a realistic serving ratio, with yakiniku sauce sodium and sugar contributions incorporated from USDA Branded Food reference data. GI is assigned by analogy to the dominant carbohydrate source (short-grain white rice) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 because vitamin D is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy source entries used for this composite dish (NDB 23620 grilled beef and NDB 20444 cooked short-grain white rice do not include vitamin D values); this sentinel value is therefore justified as missing data per the approved source. All other per_100g fields are numeric values derived from component-level USDA SR Legacy data. Sodium is elevated due to yakiniku sauce (soy sauce base); actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly depending on sauce quantity used. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included irrelevant tags (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Drinks & Beverages, Fried Chicken) which have been disregarded as they do not describe this dish. The food_id GD000208 is preserved from the input. Serving size of 400 g is a realistic estimate for a standard restaurant bowl portion at Sukiya-style establishments. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (72 × 98.0 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 70.6.
